text: Photocatalytic production of hydrogen peroxide ($H_2O_2$) from water ($H_2O$) and dioxygen ($O_2$) represents a promising strategy for generating a sustainable solar fuel that can be easily stored and utilized in fuel cells. This study demonstrates the visible-light-driven production of $H_2O_2$ in acidic aqueous media by coupling a ruthenium-based photosensitizer, $[Ru^{II}(Me_2phen)_3]^{2+}$, with various water oxidation catalysts (WOCs). Under photoirradiation, the excited state of the ruthenium complex undergoes oxidative quenching by $O_2$ to generate a superoxide radical anion ($O_2^{\bullet–}$), which subsequently disproportionates to form $H_2O_2$. The resulting $[Ru^{III}(Me_2phen)_3]^{3+}$ species is then reduced back to its initial state by $H_2O$ with the assistance of $Ir(OH)_3$ nanoparticles acting as a WOC. To enhance performance, the addition of $Sc^{3+}$ as a Lewis acid was found to effectively inhibit the back electron transfer between $[Ru^{III}(Me_2phen)_3]^{3+}$ and $O_2^{\bullet–}$, as confirmed by transient absorption and EPR spectroscopy. Replacing the $Ir(OH)_3$ nanoparticles with a homogeneous cobalt complex, $[Co^{III}(Cp^*)(bpy)(H_2O)]^{2+}$, further optimized the system, yielding a quantum yield of 37% at 450 nm and a solar-to-chemical energy conversion efficiency of 0.25%. These results provide a robust framework for the carbon-free production of $H_2O_2$ using solar energy and abundant chemical feedstocks.
